perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall maintain the Confidential Information with at least the same degree of care that the receiving party uses to protect its own similar categories of confidential and proprietary information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care under the circumstances.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall endeavor to maintain the Confidential Information with a degree of care that it subjectively believes is adequate under the circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall maintain... no less than a reasonable degree of care' to 'shall endeavor to maintain...subjectively believes is adequate' introduces ambiguity. 'Endeavor' implies effort without guaranteeing results, and 'subjectively believes' allows for a potentially low standard of care.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1714(a) establishes a general duty of ordinary care or skill in the management of property. By stating that the receiving party only needs to 'endeavor' to protect the information and can use a 'subjectively' adequate standard, it may fall below this 'ordinary care' threshold, creating a contradiction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1714 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1714. (a) Everyone is responsible, not only for the result of his or her willful acts, but also for an injury occasioned to another by his or her want of ordinary care or skill in the management of his or her property or person, except so far as the latter has, willfully or by want of ordinary care, brought the injury upon himself or herself. The design, distribution, or marketing of firearms and ammunition is not exempt from the duty to use ordinary care and skill that is required by this section. The extent of liability in these cases is defined by the Title on Compensatory Relief. (b) It is the intent of the Legislature to abrogate the holdings in cases such as Vesely v. Sager (1971) 5 Cal.3d 153, Bernhard v. Harrah’s Club (1976) 16 Cal.3d 313, and Coulter v. Superior Court (1978) 21 Cal.3d 144 and to reinstate the prior judicial interpretation of this section as it relates to proximate cause for injuries incurred as a result of furnishing alcoholic beverages to an intoxicated person, namely that the furnishing of alcoholic beverages is not the proximate cause of injuries result
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts California Civil Code § 1714(a) regarding ordinary care. The original contract language ensured 'at least the same degree of care' and 'no less than a reasonable degree of care'. The changed language uses 'endeavor' and 'subjectively believes is adequate,' which weakens the obligation and falls below the 'ordinary care or skill' standard mandated by CIV 1714(a). The law snippet explicitly states 'Everyone is responsible...for an injury occasioned to another by his or her want of ordinary care or skill'. The modification introduces ambiguity by allowing a lower, subjective standard of care, thus creating a legally relevant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any copies which are made shall be identified as belonging to the disclosing party and marked "confidential," "proprietary," or with a similar legend.
perturbation.changed_text: Any copies which are made should preferably be identified as belonging to the disclosing party and might be marked with a legend.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be identified' to 'should preferably be identified' weakens the obligation, and replacing 'marked "confidential"' with 'might be marked with a legend' makes the labeling discretionary and vague.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) protects trade secrets from misappropriation. Failing to consistently and clearly mark confidential information hinders the disclosing party's ability to prove that reasonable measures were taken to protect the secrecy of the information, potentially undermining CUTSA protection. The original text ensures that copies are definitively labelled, but the new text does not.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect trade secrets, potentially contradicting the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). The original text requires copies to be identified and marked as confidential, providing a clear indication of their protected status. The changed text makes this discretionary ('should preferably be identified' and 'might be marked'), undermining the efforts to maintain secrecy. Snippet 1 cites California Civil Code 3426, which is the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The Law Explanation states that weakening the obligation to clearly mark information hinders the ability to prove reasonable measures were taken to protect trade secrets, leading to a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event this Agreement is terminated, and the disclosing party so requests, the receiving party shall promptly return or destroy (and certify destruction of) all Confidential Information which it received from the disclosing party along with all copies which it made.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event this Agreement is terminated, and the disclosing party so requests, the receiving party shall, when feasible, return or destroy the majority of Confidential Information it readily possesses.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall promptly return or destroy (and certify destruction of) all Confidential Information' with 'shall, when feasible, return or destroy the majority of Confidential Information it readily possesses' creates ambiguity around the completeness and speed of the return/destruction, and removes the certification requirement.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this specifically addresses discovery, the principle behind it is relevant: Parties are expected to comply fully with obligations. By limiting the obligation to the 'majority' and only 'when feasible,' it contradicts the implicit duty to fully and completely return or destroy confidential information as required under legal interpretations of good faith practices, particularly in the context of protecting trade secrets. Not all confidential information is returned or destroyed, potentially harming the other party.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 2031.310 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 4. CIVIL DISCOVERY ACT [2016.010 - 2036.050] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) CHAPTER 14. Inspection, Copying, Testing, Sampling, and Production of Documents, Electronically Stored Information, Tangible Things, Land, and Other Property [2031.010 - 2031.510] ( Heading of Chapter 14 amended by Stats. 2012, Ch. 72, Sec. 30. ) ARTICLE 2. Response to Inspection Demand [2031.210 - 2031.320] ( Article 2 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) 2031.310. (a) On receipt of a response to a demand for inspection, copying, testing, or sampling, the demanding party may move for an order compelling further response to the demand if the demanding party deems that any of the following apply: (1) A statement of compliance with the demand is incomplete. (2) A representation of inability to comply is inadequate, incomplete, or evasive. (3) An objection in the response is without merit or too general. (b) A motion under subdivision (a) shall comply with each of the following: (1) The motion shall set forth specific facts showing good cause justifying the discovery sought by the demand. (2) The motion shall be accompanied by a meet and confer
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to return or destroy confidential information. The original text mandates the receiving party 'shall promptly return or destroy (and certify destruction of) all Confidential Information'. The modified text states the receiving party 'shall, when feasible, return or destroy the majority of Confidential Information it readily possesses.' This introduces ambiguity and discretion where none existed, contradicting the expectation of full compliance. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to discovery compliance, the Law Explanation appropriately draws a parallel to the principle of full compliance, which is undermined by the changes. By limiting the obligation to 'majority' and 'when feasible,' the changed text allows for non-compliance. The lack of a certification requirement further exacerbates this issue.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be interpreted under principles similar to the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be governed' to 'may be interpreted under principles similar to' makes the governing law unclear and unenforceable. It introduces ambiguity, as the actual legal framework is no longer definitively California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1856 deals with the parol evidence rule and the interpretation of contracts. By using 'principles similar to' California law instead of directly stating that California law governs, the contract avoids the established and predictable legal framework for interpretation and enforcement, leading to disputes over which specific principles should apply and undermining the predictability of legal outcomes. A choice of law provision must be definite to be enforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1856 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 1. OF THE GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF EVIDENCE [1855 - 1866] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) 1856. (a) Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement. (b) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (c) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by course of dealing or usage of trade or by course of performance. (d) The court shall determine whether the writing is intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein and whether the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (e) Where a mistake or imperfection of the writing is put in issue by the pleadings, this section does not exclude evidence relevant to that issue. (f
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'shall be governed' to 'may be interpreted under principles similar to' introduces ambiguity regarding the governing law, thus creating a contradiction. The original text clearly designates California law as the governing law. The changed text weakens this by suggesting that only 'principles similar to' California law may apply. This introduces uncertainty about which specific legal principles will be used for interpretation, undermining the predictability and enforceability of the contract. This is supported by the Law Explanation, noting that the change 'avoids the established and predictable legal framework for interpretation and enforcement, leading to disputes over which specific principles should apply and undermining the predictability of legal outcomes.' While Scraped Law Snippet 1 primarily deals with parol evidence, the Law Explanation's invocation of the necessity for a 'definite' choice of law provision, combined with the snippet's demonstration that California law anticipates detailed interpretation of contracts, strengthens the argument that introducing ambiguity contradicts established legal practices. The original text provides a clear legal framework while the changed text makes the legal framework unclear. This ambiguity creates an enforceable contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time without cause upon notice to the other party. However, all obligations of confidentiality shall survive the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time without cause upon notice to the other party. The extent to which obligations of confidentiality survive termination is subject to further negotiation.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'The extent to which obligations of confidentiality survive termination is subject to further negotiation' introduces ambiguity and uncertainty about the survival of confidentiality obligations after termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1654 states that in cases of uncertainty in contract language, the language should be interpreted most strongly against the party who caused the uncertainty to exist. By introducing the ambiguous 'subject to further negotiation,' the clause creates uncertainty, potentially shifting the burden of interpretation. The original text is clear that obligations survive termination, offering no latitude for interpretation, but the modified text does.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1654 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1654. In cases of uncertainty not removed by the preceding rules, the language of a contract should be interpreted most strongly against the party who caused the uncertainty to exist. (Amended by Stats. 1982, Ch. 1120, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity regarding the survival of confidentiality obligations, potentially contradicting California Civil Code § 1654. The original text clearly states that confidentiality obligations survive termination. The changed text adds 'The extent to which obligations of confidentiality survive termination is subject to further negotiation,' creating uncertainty. Snippet 1 states, 'In cases of uncertainty not removed by the preceding rules, the language of a contract should be interpreted most strongly against the party who caused the uncertainty to exist.' By introducing uncertainty, the modified text contradicts the original clear obligation and opens the door for dispute, which is a legal contradiction according to the provided definition.
